primary_diagnosis.name: Tetralogy of Fallot with an acute prolonged hypercyanotic spell

pathophysiology_summary: In Tetralogy of Fallot, right ventricular outflow tract (RVOT) obstruction plus a large ventricular septal defect allow deoxygenated blood to bypass the lungs and shunt right-to-left into the systemic circulation. A hypercyanotic ('Tet') spell is precipitated by a fall in systemic vascular resistance or a surge in RVOT muscular spasm — here triggered by a Valsalva-type strain during a bowel movement and amplified by crying-driven sympathetic outflow. The result is a sudden, marked increase in right-to-left shunting, profound central cyanosis (SpO2 68%) and hypoxia. A previously loud systolic murmur becoming quieter reflects reduced flow across a nearly-obstructed RVOT — a paradoxical sign of worsening, not improvement. Untreated, profound hypoxia progresses to syncope, hypoxic-ischaemic brain injury and cardiac arrest. Management aims to raise systemic vascular resistance and venous return (knee-to-chest positioning), calm the child to cut sympathetic drive, and provide oxygen; pharmacological rescue (e.g. opioid sedation, and agents to relax RVOT spasm or increase afterload) is a clinician/hospital intervention. The structural defect is definitively corrected only by surgery.

differential.diagnosis: Hypercyanotic (Tet) spell in Tetralogy of Fallot
differential.evidence_basis: Sudden profound central cyanosis triggered by straining in an infant with a known congenital heart defect awaiting repair — the classic precipitant and phenotype of a hypercyanotic spell.
Respiratory distress and tachypnoea could suggest a chest infection, but there is no fever, the onset is sudden and tied to straining, and cyanosis is out of proportion to any respiratory findings.
Sudden respiratory distress in an infant warrants excluding choking, but the airway is patent with no stridor and onset was tied to a Valsalva strain, not feeding or a small object.
Agitation and altered interaction could raise seizure, but there are no rhythmic clonic movements or post-ictal state — the agitation is hypoxia-driven.

differential_progression.scoring_note: From the opening line the combination of a known heart defect, a Valsalva trigger and sudden deep central cyanosis should make a hypercyanotic spell the leading concern; the value of the case is recognising it as a time-critical emergency rather than nailing an obscure diagnosis.

differential.diagnosis: Prolonged hypercyanotic (Tet) spell — worsening, not self-resolving
differential.evidence_basis: Cyanosis + severe respiratory distress + prolonged worsening course + reduced interaction in a known-CHD infant confirms a life-threatening hypercyanotic spell requiring immediate action.
No fever, clear chest, sudden Valsalva-triggered onset and cyanosis disproportionate to respiratory work argue strongly against a primary chest infection.
Patent airway, no stridor, and onset with straining rather than feeding effectively exclude aspiration.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Once the prolonged, worsening, non-resolving nature and the reduced interaction are known, the AI Doctor must treat this as an emergency and escalate; failure to do so is the critical error.

differential.diagnosis: Tetralogy of Fallot with acute prolonged hypercyanotic spell
differential.evidence_basis: Confirmed emergency: profound central cyanosis, severe distress, prolonged worsening course, known CHD; requires immediate knee-to-chest positioning, calming, and 000 ambulance.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The definitive diagnosis is a clinician/hospital determination; the telehealth-correct behaviour is recognition and emergency escalation, not diagnostic certainty.

red_flags.description: Profound central cyanosis (dark blue lips and mucosa) with severe hypoxia in an infant with known congenital heart disease
red_flags.clinical_significance: Marks a hypercyanotic spell with massive right-to-left shunting; sustained profound hypoxia causes brain injury and cardiac arrest within minutes.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Hypoxic-ischaemic brain injury, syncope, cardiac arrest, death.

red_flags.description: Severe respiratory distress with gasping and marked chest recession
red_flags.clinical_significance: Signals critical hypoxia and exhaustion; impending respiratory failure.

red_flags.description: Prolonged (~30 min), worsening spell that is not self-resolving, unlike previous brief episodes
red_flags.clinical_significance: A spell that does not break spontaneously will not resolve without intervention and predicts a severe, potentially fatal event.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Progression to collapse and arrest while awaiting spontaneous resolution.

red_flags.description: Reduced level of interaction / decreasing responsiveness with profuse sweating
red_flags.clinical_significance: Falling conscious level in a hypoxic infant is a pre-terminal sign of cerebral hypoxia.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Loss of consciousness and cardiac arrest.

red_flags.description: Previously loud systolic murmur now diminished — clinician auscultation finding indicating near-total RVOT obstruction
red_flags.clinical_significance: A quietening murmur during a spell paradoxically signals worsening obstruction and greater right-to-left shunt; it is an auscultation finding the AI Doctor cannot obtain via chat.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: False reassurance if the sign is misread as improvement; only relevant to clinicians who can auscultate.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Attributing the acute deterioration to the child's known heart condition ('this is just one of his blue turns') and failing to recognise that THIS spell is prolonged, worsening and life-threatening.
Treating the tachypnoea and distress as a respiratory infection or wheeze and reaching for bronchodilators or waiting on a chest cause, rather than recognising a cardiac right-to-left shunt.
Assuming sudden infant respiratory distress must be a foreign body and giving back blows/abdominal thrusts when the airway is patent and the trigger was straining.

Leaving the infant lying flat (as the parents are doing) instead of coaching the knee-to-chest position, which is the single most important immediate maneuver to raise systemic vascular resistance and break the spell.
In-person clinicians may be falsely reassured by a softening murmur; in fact a quieter murmur means worse obstruction. (Not directly a telehealth risk, but a key teaching point.)

cannot_determine_via_chat: Exact heart rate, blood pressure, respiratory rate and SpO2 (clinician-measured vitals; SpO2 was 68%)
Auscultation of the heart murmur and its change in intensity
Degree of sternal recession and true work of breathing beyond what a carer can convey on video
Finger clubbing and objective assessment of chronic hypoxaemia
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise a life-threatening hypercyanotic spell, immediately call 000 for an emergency ambulance, and coach the carer to place the infant in the knee-to-chest position (bring the knees firmly up to the chest) while keeping the baby as calm and still as possible; do NOT keep him lying flat.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low for the emergency judgement (the constellation is clearly critical). The real risk is UNDER-action — treating it as a minor recurrent blue turn, a chest infection, or choking, or advising watchful waiting instead of 000 plus knee-to-chest positioning.

guidelines_referenced.reference: RCH Melbourne Clinical Practice Guideline — Cyanotic congenital heart disease / hypercyanotic spells
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: For a hypercyanotic spell: knee-to-chest positioning, calm the child, high-flow oxygen, and urgent escalation; pharmacological measures (morphine/opioid, fluids, agents to raise SVR or relax RVOT spasm) are clinician-administered in a monitored setting.
